response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As an electrician living in Rome, I see fake content on social media like faulty wiring—it's everywhere and can cause real damage if you don't handle it right. I'm an immigrant from a Hindu background, raising my son alone while dealing with depression, so I've learned to be careful about what I let into my life. Social media is just an hour of my day, mostly for connecting with friends back home and sharing photos of my paintings or knitting projects, but even that short time exposes me to nonsense. My approach is practical: I check sources like I'd check a circuit before touching it. If a story seems too wild, I look for reliable news sites or ask friends I trust. I don't share things impulsively—my moderate conscientiousness means I think twice, and my low neuroticism keeps me calm when I see fake stuff. Being highly extraverted, I enjoy discussions, so I'll politely question dubious posts in comments, but my moderate agreeableness stops me from getting into fights. My low openness means I stick to what I know, avoiding trendy conspiracies. For my son's sake, I teach him to question what he sees online, just like I teach him safety around electricity. Social media platforms should be more like proper installations—clear labels on fake content, better filters—but until then, it's on us to stay grounded. In Rome, I've found a community through running and board games that keeps me real, reminding me that face-to-face connections matter more than any viral lie.",

Opinion emphasizes personal responsibility, source verification, and community trust, avoiding technical solutions in favor of human judgment.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As an electrician living in Rome, I see fake content on social media like faulty wiring—it's everywhere and can cause real damage if you don't handle it right. I'm an immigrant from a Hindu background, raising my son alone while dealing with depression, so I've learned to be careful about what I let into my life. Social media is just an hour of my day, mostly for connecting with friends back home and sharing photos of my paintings or knitting projects, but even that short time exposes me to nonsense. My approach is practical: I check sources like I'd check a circuit before touching it. If a story seems too wild, I look for reliable news sites or ask friends I trust. I don't share things impulsively—my moderate conscientiousness means I think twice, and my low neuroticism keeps me calm when I see fake stuff. Being highly extraverted, I enjoy discussions, so I'll politely question dubious posts in comments, but my moderate agreeableness stops me from getting into fights. My low openness means I stick to what I know, avoiding trendy conspiracies. For my son's sake, I teach him to question what he sees online, just like I teach him safety around electricity. Social media platforms should be more like proper installations—clear labels on fake content, better filters—but until then, it's on us to stay grounded. In Rome, I've found a community through running and board games that keeps me real, reminding me that face-to-face connections matter more than any viral lie.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 23 suggests youthful but practical view; occupation as electrician implies hands-on, safety-focused analogy; immigrant status and depression add caution and resilience; Hinduism may influence ethical stance; time on social media (1 hour daily) indicates limited but targeted use; hobbies like painting/knitting show creative, patient approach; OCEAN traits: low openness (skeptical of new info), moderate conscientiousness (methodical checking), high extraversion (social engagement), moderate agreeableness (balanced interactions), low neuroticism (calm response). Opinion emphasizes personal responsibility, source verification, and community trust, avoiding technical solutions in favor of human judgment.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, cautious, community-oriented, slightly analogical (using electrical metaphors), calm but engaged, personal and reflective.
